macro_segments.title: Introduction to Shechita Shelo Lishmo and the Four Avodot

micro_segments.title: Opening context: Pesach korban and scheduling on Erev Pesach
Comparison to Zevachim: the rule of shelo lishmo in korbanot
The general principle: korban shelo lishmo is kosher as an object but invalid for the owner's obligation
The exception of chatat: shelo lishmo invalidates completely because it requires a specific owner relationship
Application to Pesach: like chatat, shelo lishmo invalidates because eating is central to Pesach's purpose
Distinction between pigul and shelo lishmo: different types of invalid intention
Note on articulated vs. unarticulated thought in intention
Reading the Mishnah: the four avodot (shechita, kabbalah, holachah, zrika) and their relevance
The Mishnah's presentation: shelo lishmo at any avodah invalidates, whether pure or mixed intention
Examples of mixed intention: Pesach with Shelamim, Shelamim with Pesach

macro_segments.title: Rav Pappa's Question: One Avodah versus Two Avodot in Hybrid Intention

micro_segments.title: Rav Pappa's question: are the two thoughts during one act or two separate acts?
Rabbi Meir vs. Rabbi Yose dispute: beginning only vs. entire statement
Application to shechita: if one act with hybrid thought, debate between Rabbi Meir and Rabbi Yose
If two avodot: both Rabbi Meir and Rabbi Yose agree it's invalid
Gemarah's focus: determining which scenario the Mishnah addresses
Clarification: if shelo lishmo comes first, everyone agrees it's invalid immediately

macro_segments.title: Reading the Mishnah: Distinguishing First Case from Hybrid Case

micro_segments.title: First case of Mishnah: shechita shelo lishmo followed by correct subsequent avodot
Vav as 'or' not 'and': reading the Mishnah's structure
Proof: the first case already covers different thoughts at different avodot
Therefore the hybrid case must mean two thoughts during one avodah
Rebuttal: alternative reading where all cases involve separate avodot
Distinguishing first case: thought during the act vs. thought about future acts
The hybrid case: shechita for Pesach with intention to throw blood for Shelamim
This teaches machshavah from one avodah to another is relevant
Gemarah's conclusion: Rav Pappa's question remains unresolved in terms of the Mishnah's reading

macro_segments.title: The Owners (Le'ochlav) Case: Mixed Intention Regarding Eaters

micro_segments.title: Transition to second Mishnah: intention regarding wrong owners differs from wrong korban
Basic rule: wrong owners alone invalidate, but mixed owners is kosher
First case: shechita shelo l'ochlav and shelo limnuyav
Clarification: le'ochlav can mean unable to eat; limnuyav means different group
This case is clearly one avodah (shechita)
Proof attempt: since seifa is one avodah, reisha must also be one avodah
Rebuttal: each Mishnah stands on its own; reisha could be two avodot

macro_segments.title: Principle of Machshevet Ochlim: Thought About Eaters Only Relevant During Shechita

micro_segments.title: Second proof: if different avodot with thought at zrika about wrong eaters, why is it kosher?
Central principle: ein machshevet ochlim b'zrika—thought about eaters is irrelevant during blood-throwing
Uniqueness of Pesach: eating is central; relevance of eaters is only at shechita
Connection to Torah: ish lefi ochlo tachosu al haseh—counting eaters determines shechita
If hybrid thought of eaters during one avodah (shechita), would be invalid by other principles
Implication: mixed thought of eaters is valid only because bad thought wasn't at shechita
Tradition: mixed thought of eaters is not problematic, unlike mixed shelo lishmo
Conclusion: seifa refers to mixed thought during one avodah (shechita only)
Therefore reisha also discusses mixed thought during one avodah
Final rebuttal: each Mishnah uses different parameters; still no definitive answer

macro_segments.title: The Puzzle of Pesach on Other Days: Can Shelo Lishmo Validate Mixed Intention?

micro_segments.title: Introduction: Pesach shechted on Purim (not Erev Pesach) can become Shelamim if intended
The question: if shechted with mixed intention (Pesach and Shelamim), what is status?
The tension: shelo lishmo (Shelamim) makes it valid, but lishmo (Pesach) makes it invalid
Which wins: in hybrid thought, does bad thought always win or does context matter?
Rav Dimi's argument: shelo lishmo is universal; lishmo is specific to Pesach; thus shelo lishmo doesn't overcome mixed thought
Conclusion not reached; Rava will now address it

macro_segments.title: Rava's Position: Shelo Lishmo Wins Out Even in Mixed Intention on Purim

micro_segments.title: Rava's conclusion: Pesach shechted on Purim with mixed intention is kosher
Analogy: normally when you think Shelamim on Purim it overrides the Pesach designation
The Pesach itself represents Pesach status; even so, shelo lishmo (Shelamim) wins out
Therefore: if shelo lishmo overcomes the korban's own status, it will overcome mixed thought too
Shelo lishmo trumps even to validate (here it validates; in Mishnah it invalidates)
Therefore mixed intention doesn't matter; shelo lishmo still wins

macro_segments.title: Rav Adda's Objection: Speaking Differently from Remaining Silent

micro_segments.title: Rav Adda challenges: maybe saying both thoughts weakens your statement vs. saying one
Proof from le'ochlav: if you say only shelo l'ochlav it's invalid, not rescued by Pesach's status
Even though Pesach naturally represents the right eaters, your statement of wrong eaters isn't combined with it
The distinction: when you say something vs. when you're silent, the Pesach doesn't bail you out
Here too: when you say a mixed thought, it's weaker than when you say one thing
Rav Adda concludes: you can't use the Pesach's status to supplement your own hybrid statement

macro_segments.title: Rava's Response: The Eaters Case Is Different Because Status Is Mutable

micro_segments.title: Rava rejects the comparison: the eaters case is different
In eaters case: the group can change; people withdraw and join until shechita
Pesach's status regarding eaters is not fixed; therefore you need to articulate owners explicitly
On Purim: the Pesach has only one immutable identity—a Pesach korban
When you say Shelamim against a fully committed Pesach status, it wins
Therefore even saying both thoughts, shelo lishmo still wins because it overcomes the fixed status
Debate ends: Rava's explanation emphasizes the immutability of Pesach status on Purim vs. mutability of eaters
